network.overall_strategy: The network is literature-constrained and therefore treats abiotic proline formation as robust, but L-proline as a limited-confidence endpoint reached through speculative prebiotic enrichment/selection from a racemic proline pool rather than a demonstrated direct asymmetric synthesis. Ten pathways are generated by combining four literature-supported source branches—(i) spark discharge product mixtures, (ii) cyanosulfidic photochemistry via the Patel-Sutherland proline aminonitrile route, (iii) HCN oligomer/polymer hydrolysis chemistry, and (iv) protometabolic alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate to glutamic-gamma-semialdehyde to P5C to proline chemistry—with cross-environment transfer between hydrothermal, surface, and biochemical settings. Hub molecules are HCN, alpha-ketoglutarate, glutamate, P5C, and racemic proline. Where the literature identifies unresolved steps, especially glutamate gamma-carboxyl selective reduction and aqueous prebiotic enantioenrichment to L-proline, those reactions are explicitly marked as speculative in the reasoning.

molecules.iupac_name: pyrrolidine-2-carboxylic acid in spark-discharge product mixture
glutamic acid in spark-discharge product mixture

reactions.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic formation of alpha-ketoglutarate from CO2 under iron-sulfur catalysis

conditions.catalyst: pyrite and greigite iron-sulfur mineral network
conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal/serpentinizing setting; H2-rich; reaction is a network-level condensation abstraction rather than a single demonstrated elementary step

reactions.mechanism: Iron-sulfur-driven CO2 reduction and protometabolic carboxylation/condensation chemistry generate TCA-like alpha-keto acid intermediates, converging on alpha-ketoglutarate.
reactions.reasoning: Alpha-ketoglutarate is identified in pyrite-driven protometabolic TCA analog chemistry and in nonenzymatic keto-acid networks, but not as a simple single-step CO2+H2 product. This reaction is therefore a literature-grounded network abstraction connecting hydrothermal carbon fixation to the glutamate family rather than a claim of one-step synthesis (Muchowska et al., 2017; Stubbs et al., 2020; Springsteen et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Nickel-catalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate

conditions.pressure: 1-10 bar H2 to high local hydrothermal pressure
conditions.catalyst: Ni-bearing catalytic surface represented in-network by Fe0/FeS2 hydrothermal mineral context
conditions.other: pH about 8 in mild catalytic studies; hydrothermal mineral surfaces provide reducing context

reactions.mechanism: Reductive amination of the alpha-keto group with ammonia/hydrogen yields glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids is strongly supported on sulfide/mineral catalysts, and a recent prebiotic Krebs analog specifically generated glutamate from keto-acid chemistry under H2/NH3 with nickel catalysts. The present reaction uses the hydrothermal Fe-rich mineral setting required by the schema while retaining the literature-supported chemistry (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1998; Huber & Wachtershauser, 2003; Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Partial reduction of glutamate to glutamic-gamma-semialdehyde

conditions.other: alkaline vent-like reducing environment; selective gamma-carboxyl reduction is an unresolved bottleneck

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-assisted partial reduction of the side-chain carboxyl group of glutamate gives the semialdehyde without full reduction of the alpha-carboxyl group.
reactions.reasoning: The glutamate to GSA transformation is the key prebiotic bottleneck highlighted in the literature. It is included because the glutamate to P5C to proline route is strongly emphasized, but the selective reduction itself remains chemically demanding and incompletely demonstrated; Fe2+/FeS/H2S systems are proposed as plausible reductants rather than experimentally established full solutions (Vogel & Davis, 1952; Strecker, 1957; Huber & Wachtershauser, 1998; review synthesis in the provided report).

reactions.name: Spontaneous cyclization of glutamic-gamma-semialdehyde to P5C

conditions.other: nonenzymatic intramolecular imine formation; no mineral required

reactions.mechanism: Intramolecular condensation between the amino group and aldehyde closes the five-membered ring to the imine/enamine tautomer set known as P5C.
reactions.reasoning: This is one of the strongest steps in the entire network: GSA spontaneously cyclizes in aqueous solution to Delta1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate without enzyme catalysis, furnishing the pyrrolidine precursor skeleton central to proline chemistry (Vogel & Davis, 1952).

reactions.name: Reduction of P5C to racemic proline

conditions.other: Fe2+, FeS/H2S, or H2 from serpentinization proposed as reductants

reactions.mechanism: Reduction of the cyclic imine of P5C furnishes proline.
reactions.reasoning: The P5C to proline reduction is chemically plausible in prebiotic reducing environments and follows the known biosynthetic logic, but the exact abiotic reducing system remains less directly demonstrated than the cyclization step. It is therefore included as a plausible literature-based completion of the glutamate-family route to racemic proline (Strecker, 1957; hydrothermal reductive context discussed in Russell & Hall, 1997, 2006 and the provided report).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge generation of HCN in reduced atmospheric mixtures

conditions.temperature: electrical discharge with bulk aqueous trap near ambient
conditions.catalyst: surface concentration on TiO2/clay after atmospheric production
conditions.other: represents reduced atmospheric spark chemistry using CH4, N2, H2O with trace NH3; network input is compressed to NH3-containing reduced atmosphere because CH4 and N2 are outside the molecule set

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge in reduced gas mixtures generates cyanide species that dissolve into surface waters and are concentrated on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: HCN is a canonical product/feedstock of Miller-Urey-type reduced atmospheric chemistry. This reaction is explicitly a source-term abstraction for the spark literature and is not intended as NH3-only chemistry; the reasoning retains the actual reported gas mixtures. Mineral agents are included because the schema requires mineral specification for surface reactions, representing adsorption/concentration rather than the discharge itself (Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Jiang et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge direct formation of a racemic proline pool

conditions.temperature: electrical discharge with aqueous trap near ambient
conditions.catalyst: mineral adsorption and preservation surfaces in collection environment
conditions.other: abstraction of CH4/N2/H2O plus trace NH3 spark experiments; proline is a minor but reproducible product; secondary-amine analytical underreporting noted

reactions.mechanism: Complex atmospheric plasma chemistry generates amino acid mixtures including proline that accumulate in aqueous condensates.
reactions.reasoning: Proline was definitively identified in classic spark-discharge experiments and later reanalyses, although some modern derivatization methods underreport it because proline is a secondary amine. This node captures the literature-supported existence of a direct racemic proline source without asserting a fully resolved mechanism (Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Jiang et al., 2014; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge formation of a glutamate-containing amino acid pool

conditions.temperature: electrical discharge with aqueous trap near ambient
conditions.other: abstraction of reduced gas discharge experiments yielding glutamic acid among amino acid products

reactions.mechanism: Spark chemistry produces mixed amino acids including glutamate/glutamic acid in aqueous condensates.
reactions.reasoning: Glutamic acid is among amino acids reported in spark discharge and meteoritic suites; this reaction provides a literature-supported surface source feeding the glutamate-family branch without duplicating the glutamate molecule node (Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Kvenvolden et al., 1970).

reactions.name: Assimilation of spark-derived glutamate pool into shared glutamate hub

conditions.other: transport/mixing node connecting surface and hydrothermal-biochemical chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Physical transfer and pooling of chemically identical glutamate species across environments.
reactions.reasoning: This connector is necessary to satisfy molecule uniqueness and cross-environment network connectivity. It is not a chemical transformation but an environmental pooling step grounded in the fact that glutamate from different sources is chemically identical (network-connecting abstraction; justified by the reported occurrence of glutamate in spark and abiotic settings).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN

conditions.other: anoxic; concentrated cyanide solutions; oligomerization of 4 HCN equivalents

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes oligomerization to the tetramer diaminomaleonitrile, a key branch-point cyanide polymer intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is a canonical HCN oligomer and central intermediate in prebiotic cyanide chemistry. Its inclusion directly follows the HCN oligomerization literature and the review identifying DAMN/polymer pathways as contributors to amino acid formation (Oró, 1961; Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Conversion of DAMN/polymeric HCN chemistry to hydantoin-type proline precursor

conditions.other: represents multistep HCN polymer rearrangement/hydrolysis; condition-sensitive and low-specificity

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomers/polymer fragments rearrange and hydrolyze through heterocyclic intermediates including hydantoins that can serve as amino acid precursors.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports proline among some HCN polymer hydrolysis products, but the pathway is complex and likely passes through hydantoin-like intermediates rather than a direct DAMN-to-proline jump. This reaction is included specifically to avoid overcompression criticized in earlier attempts (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013; Oró, 1961).

reactions.name: Hydrolytic release of racemic proline from hydantoin-type precursor

conditions.other: mixed-product hydrolysis; low selectivity

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of heterocyclic amino acid precursor liberates racemic proline.
reactions.reasoning: Amino acid release from HCN-derived hydantoin/polymer chemistry is supported in the review literature, with proline reported among products under some conditions. This step is therefore plausible but lower-specificity than the cyanosulfidic route (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: HCN hydration/photochemistry to formamide

conditions.temperature: UV-exposed shallow pools; ambient to warm
conditions.other: anoxic; UV irradiation

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide-bearing surface waters under UV evolve toward formamide-containing pools that participate in broader prebiotic synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is a recognized prebiotic intermediate in UV-driven surface chemistry, and TiO2-catalyzed formamide photochemistry has been linked to amino acid formation. This step provides an additional surface route into mixed amino acid pools while remaining literature-based (Saladino et al., 2017; broader UV cyanide/formamide prebiotic literature summarized in the report).

reactions.name: TiO2-photocatalyzed formamide chemistry contributing to racemic proline pool

conditions.temperature: UV-exposed shallow pools, ambient to warm
conditions.other: mixed amino acid-forming regime; product specificity for proline is low

reactions.mechanism: Photocatalytic formamide decomposition and C-N bond-forming chemistry generate mixed amino acid products including proline as a minor component.
reactions.reasoning: TiO2/formamide photochemistry is reported to produce amino acids under prebiotic conditions. The assignment to proline is lower-confidence and should be viewed as an auxiliary minor source to the racemic proline pool rather than a selective route, included because the literature review specifically mentions TiO2/formamide amino acid synthesis (Saladino et al., 2017).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic generation of 4-carbon cyanohydrin precursor

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox in phosphate buffer represented alongside required mineral agents
conditions.other: neutral pH, anoxic, UV irradiation; shallow surface pools

reactions.mechanism: HCN/H2S photoredox chemistry under neutral anoxic UV-exposed conditions builds reduced cyanohydrin precursors on a 4-carbon scaffold.
reactions.reasoning: This step follows the Patel-Sutherland cyanosulfidic framework for amino acid precursor generation at 35C, neutral pH, under UV with Cu photoredox cycling and phosphate buffer. The exact species are represented at the network level as the required 4-carbon cyanohydrin precursor leading to proline movement c (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic sulfur incorporation and cyclization to cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system in neutral phosphate-buffered water
conditions.other: anoxic; UV irradiation

reactions.mechanism: Reaction of the 4-carbon cyanohydrin with sulfide yields open-chain and cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide species; the cyclic branch is captured here.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. specifically identify the cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide as the crucial ring-forming intermediate on the proline branch. This route is the most mechanistically detailed prebiotic path to proline in the literature (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: UV-driven deoxygenation/cyclization to gamma-butyrothiolactam

conditions.catalyst: UV photochemistry with Cu photoredox context
conditions.other: neutral pH; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical deoxygenation and ring-constraining sulfur chemistry convert the cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide into gamma-butyrothiolactam.
reactions.reasoning: The literature emphasizes that proline's ring emerges naturally at the thioamide/thiolactam stage in the cyanosulfidic network. This specific intermediate is central to movement c in the Patel-Sutherland scheme (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Photoreduction and cyanide addition to proline aminonitrile

conditions.other: neutral pH; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Photoreductive transformation of the thiolactam followed by HCN incorporation yields the aminonitrile precursor of proline.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. identify formation of the proline aminonitrile precursor after gamma-butyrothiolactam under the same cyanosulfidic photochemical regime. This is one of the strongest experimentally grounded sequences for abiotic proline synthesis (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of proline aminonitrile to racemic proline

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis converts the aminonitrile into the amino acid carboxylate, furnishing proline.
reactions.reasoning: Aminonitrile hydrolysis to amino acids is standard prebiotic logic and completes the Patel-Sutherland route to proline. No compelling literature support exists for intrinsic enantioselectivity at this step, so the product is represented as racemic proline (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Pooling of spark-discharge proline into shared racemic proline hub

conditions.other: mixing/transport step

reactions.mechanism: Environmental transfer merges chemically identical proline from spark products into the common proline pool.
reactions.reasoning: This is a connectivity step needed because direct spark products and downstream surface/hydrothermal products must converge onto a single unique proline molecule node. It is justified by the direct observational literature for spark-discharge proline (Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Jiang et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Speculative prebiotic enrichment/selection of L-proline from racemic proline

conditions.other: speculative combination of crystallization/eutectic partitioning, mineral adsorption, or organocatalytic feedback; not demonstrated under fully prebiotic aqueous conditions

reactions.mechanism: Physical-chemical symmetry breaking and amplification enrich one enantiomer from the racemate, here represented as L-proline.
reactions.reasoning: The literature is explicit that robust prebiotic aqueous generation of homochiral L-proline has not been demonstrated. This reaction is therefore speculative but necessary because the requested target is L-proline. It is motivated by proline-specific eutectic amplification in nonprebiotic solvents and by broader discussions of CPL, chiral mineral adsorption, and amplification mechanisms, none of which yet solve the aqueous prebiotic problem for proline (Hayashi et al., 2006; Klussmann et al., 2006; Bailey et al., 1998; Meinert et al., 2014; Hazen et al., 2001; List et al., 2000; Mathew et al., 2004).

pathways.name: Hydrothermal alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate-family route
pathways.description: Protometabolic hydrothermal carbon fixation generates alpha-ketoglutarate, which is reductively aminated to glutamate, partially reduced to GSA, spontaneously cyclized to P5C, reduced to racemic proline, then speculatively enriched to L-proline.

pathways.name: Spark-derived glutamate feeding the glutamate-family branch
pathways.description: Surface spark chemistry supplies glutamate directly, which is transferred into the shared glutamate hub and proceeds through GSA and P5C to proline and then speculative L-selection.

pathways.name: Direct spark-discharge proline route
pathways.description: A reduced atmospheric spark-discharge product mixture generates proline directly; this racemic proline pool is then transferred into the common proline hub and subjected to speculative L-enrichment.

pathways.name: HCN oligomerization to hydantoin-mediated proline route
pathways.description: Spark-supplied HCN oligomerizes to DAMN, progresses through hydantoin-type precursors, hydrolyzes to racemic proline, then enters the speculative enrichment step.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic proline aminonitrile route
pathways.description: HCN/H2S/UV chemistry in shallow anoxic pools forms a 4-carbon cyanohydrin, cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide, gamma-butyrothiolactam, and proline aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to racemic proline before speculative L-enrichment.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface integrated route via HCN and cyanosulfidic chemistry
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reducing environments and surface spark chemistry jointly supply HCN feedstock that enters the Patel-Sutherland branch to proline aminonitrile and then proline.

pathways.name: Spark HCN to formamide/TiO2 mixed amino-acid route
pathways.description: Surface cyanide pools are transformed to formamide under UV and TiO2 photocatalysis, contributing a mixed amino-acid output that includes racemic proline before speculative L-selection.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal alpha-ketoglutarate plus surface glutamate convergence route
pathways.description: Distinct hydrothermal and surface sources converge on the shared glutamate hub; once pooled, the chemistry proceeds through the spontaneous P5C ring-closing branch to proline.

pathways.name: Dual-surface convergence on racemic proline
pathways.description: Independent surface branches—direct spark-discharge proline and HCN oligomer/hydantoin chemistry—both feed the same racemic proline pool before speculative L-enrichment.

pathways.name: Multi-source convergent L-proline endpoint
pathways.description: Hydrothermal glutamate-family chemistry, cyanosulfidic aminonitrile chemistry, and direct spark/HCN polymer routes all converge on the common racemic proline hub, from which only a speculative enrichment step reaches L-proline.
